cohort.label: Initial 50-jurisdiction bilateral-agreement research cohort
cohort.basis: Union of all 41 passport jurisdictions or territories named in the European Commission's 2019 Article 20(2)(b) notification and nine high-demand control markets visible in official 2024 tourism or 2025 visa-demand statistics.
cohort.limitation: No public official EU-wide top 50 by passport or citizenship exists. Eurostat measures country of residence and publishes only a limited set of individual non-EU origins; visa demand is by consulate location. Neither metric identifies the passport used.

currentVerifications.summary: Austria's current government table lists a national exemption of three calendar months for Israeli ordinary passports; the treaty text limits the stay to non-employment purposes.
currentVerifications.territorialScope: Austria only while the national bilateral period is invoked.
currentVerifications.sequencingRule: The Austrian EES procedure permits a request on entry or during the stay, no later than the last working day of the ordinary Schengen 90/180 allowance.
currentVerifications.authorityProcedure: The traveller must request the bilateral stay from the competent Landespolizeidirektion so it can be recorded in EES. Late regularisation requires credible proof that the traveller stayed only in Austria.
currentVerifications.externalExitRequirement: Exit Schengen directly through an Austrian external border; onward travel through another Schengen state for exit is not permitted after the ordinary allowance is exhausted.

currentVerifications.summary: Latvia's current Foreign Ministry page explicitly lists Israel and says that, after using the ordinary Schengen allowance, a visa-exempt traveller may remain in Latvia for up to another 90 days under the bilateral agreement.
currentVerifications.territorialScope: Latvia only after the ordinary Schengen allowance is exhausted.
currentVerifications.sequencingRule: The Latvian page places the bilateral period after the ordinary Schengen 90/180 period.
currentVerifications.authorityProcedure: The Latvian page does not explain the current EES request and recording procedure. EU law requires a timely request and a host-state EES record; the traveller must obtain current instructions from the Latvian authority.
currentVerifications.externalExitRequirement: EU law requires direct exit through Latvia's external border when the bilateral period is used.

currentVerifications.summary: Current Danish Immigration Service guidance lists an old bilateral right for Israeli nationals, but it is three calendar months from first entry into Denmark or another Nordic country, with prior days in Denmark or another Nordic country during the preceding six months deducted. It is not a flat additional 90 days.
currentVerifications.territorialScope: The permitted bilateral stay is in Denmark; time in other Nordic countries affects the Danish calculation.
currentVerifications.sequencingRule: The Danish guidance describes use after a 90-day stay in another Schengen state.
currentVerifications.authorityProcedure: The Danish guidance does not state how to request and record the stay in EES. EU law requires a timely request and host-state record; the traveller must obtain current instructions from Danish authorities.
currentVerifications.externalExitRequirement: EU law requires direct exit through Denmark's external border when the bilateral period is used.

currentVerifications.summary: A live France Diplomatie page lists Israel among seven agreements allowing 90 days in metropolitan France independently of the prior ordinary Schengen stay.
currentVerifications.sequencingRule: The bilateral stay must follow, not precede, the ordinary Schengen 90-day allowance.
currentVerifications.authorityProcedure: The traveller must identify themselves when crossing the French border so a French border officer can adjust the EES record manually.
currentVerifications.externalExitRequirement: EU law requires direct exit through France's external border after using the bilateral period.

currentVerifications.summary: The 2019 Commission notification contains an Israel-Belgium candidate, but Belgium's current embassy and Immigration Office guidance states only the ordinary 90 days in 180 and does not describe a bilateral extension. Do not rely on extra time without direct written confirmation from the competent Belgian authority.
currentVerifications.territorialScope: Unresolved; current Belgian guidance does not publish an operational bilateral territorial rule.
currentVerifications.sequencingRule: Unresolved because current Belgian guidance does not publish an operational bilateral procedure.
currentVerifications.authorityProcedure: Contact the Belgian Immigration Office or border authority before relying on the 2019 notification.
currentVerifications.externalExitRequirement: No bilateral benefit should be assumed. The ordinary 90/180 rule remains the only supported calculation.

currentVerifications.summary: Polish government sources conflict: one business portal mentions Israel as a bilateral example, while the embassy and Office for Foreigners publish only the ordinary rule or omit Israel; a July 2026 Border Guard report describes refusal of an Israeli traveller after exhaustion of visa-free stay. Do not present a bilateral benefit.
currentVerifications.territorialScope: Unresolved because the current official sources conflict.
currentVerifications.sequencingRule: Unresolved because the current official sources conflict.
currentVerifications.authorityProcedure: Obtain direct written confirmation from the Polish Border Guard or Office for Foreigners before relying on any bilateral arrangement.
currentVerifications.externalExitRequirement: No bilateral benefit should be assumed. The ordinary 90/180 rule remains the only supported calculation.
